title: Against Posthumanism: Notes towards an Ethopolitics of Personhood
author: Osborne, Rose

opening_move.description: The paper opens with a direct challenge to posthumanism — both its standpoint-critical version (Braidotti) and its technological-diagnostic version — arguing that both foreclose crucial ethical and political debates that are better framed through the ethopolitics of personhood.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Posthumanism, by positing a threshold crossed or yet to be crossed, evades the empirical and political realities of how most humans actually live — constrained by material inequality, embodied vulnerability, and denied forms of personhood.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper accepts some posthumanist insights (that humans have always been augmented and tool-using) while rejecting the binary human/posthuman framework, proposing instead an ethopolitical vitalism centered on personhood and its material scaffolding.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Philosophical argument using examples from cognitive science, neurology (Oliver Sacks, Pierre Janet), law (habeas corpus cases, disability law, UN conventions), and neurodiversity activism (Amanda Baggs) as illustrative reference points — not as empirical evidence.
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper makes extensive use of scientific literature (Clark, Sterelny, Ginsburg and Jablonka) and legal examples, but as illustrations of philosophical claims rather than empirical support. Classified as fully_normative because the argument is throughout about what frame we should adopt (ethopolitics vs posthumanism) and what ethical/political responses are required.

confidence_notes: The paper is clearly fully_normative — it makes no empirical claims of its own and uses scientific/legal examples purely illustratively. The opening_move is polemical_intervention because the paper's raison d'être is explicitly to argue against posthumanism rather than to diagnose a puzzle requiring new concepts. The distinction between 'standpoint posthumanism' (Braidotti) and 'technological posthumanism' is a key conceptual_distinction that structures the rival_mapping. The counter_objection_response appears via the section on 'Uneasy Cyborgs' which explicitly anticipates and rebuts the strongest technological posthumanist claims. The conclusion's vitalism-of-pathos is meta-reflexive in qualifying what vitalism means in this context.
